lesson.menu_desc: Perfect modals + concessive clauses for arguing the punishment -- 29 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 14 -- Sentencing: arguing mitigation and aggravation at the punishment stage.
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Luiz Bressane | Aula 14 -- Sentencing

listenings.text: Once the verdict is in, your real work as a defender begins, because sentencing is where a human life is weighed. Never argue the facts again; the guilt is settled. Argue the person. Lay out the mitigating factors first: a clean record, a hard childhood, genuine remorse, a willingness to make amends. The defendant could have run, but he stayed and confessed. He might have blamed others, yet he took responsibility. Concede the aggravating factors honestly, because the judge already knows them, and hiding them costs you trust. Then ask for proportion. A sentence should fit the offence, not the anger of the moment. Remind the court that the goal is not only to punish, but to rehabilitate. The judge ought to leave a door open, and it is your job to show where that door is.

You are listening to the legal affairs review. At the sentencing hearing in the State versus Andrade, the courtroom heard two very different portraits of the same man. The prosecution stressed the aggravating circumstances: the breach of trust and the harm to the victim, arguing that the offence warranted the maximum term. The defence answered with mitigation. Counsel acknowledged the gravity, but argued that the defendant should have been offered treatment years earlier, and that he could have caused far greater harm yet chose to stop. Although the judge accepted that the crime was serious, she agreed that remorse and a clean record counted for something. The sentence handed down was custodial, but well below the maximum. Observers called it a balanced outcome. We will report on the appeal if one is filed.
